content: <@289320818901778434> <@88644018673582080> new Gen Urobuchi interview about Saya and the collab
nice
Kotori Kowai wrote the lyrics for the jp collab song

> That said, as the story progresses, there’s a plot point where she ends up killing a friend—even if it was to protect Saya. I have to admit, I can’t really relate to that part (laughs).
> 
> Urobuchi:
> It would be a real problem if you could relate to that (laughs).
> 
> Koiwai:
> You’re right. While I can’t relate to the action itself, I could relate to the “way of seeing the world” that led her to that point.
> 
> Translated with DeepL.com (free version)
lol
yeah she knows the story is still fck up
knowing that the cgs were done with a limited palette is intereting
mostly because it really dosent look like it

> Koiwai:
> Ryoko suddenly shows off her action skills, doesn’t she? I felt like the tone of the story shifted from that point on, and I found it really interesting.
> 
> Urobuchi:
> Well, I just couldn’t hold back while I was writing it. At first, I intended to make her more of a “character who screams and gets chased around.”
> 
> But as I wrote, I gradually got angry at the plot where only humans were getting killed…
> 
> I guess I’m just not cut out for horror. I kind of wanted to get some payback, you know? I suppose my roots are in action.
> 
> Ms. Koiwai:
> I was totally surprised when a real gun suddenly appeared.
> 
> Translated with DeepL.com (free version)
ryoko W

From a man's point of view, I think adolescent girls were "monsters" in particular.

I don't understand, or I don't fit in the frame of my imagination. As an adult, you can have the same perspective, share values, and have a dialogue.

However, from my point of view, girls who have not yet decided on their emotions are highly monstroic. Of course, that's also an attraction. I think it is a creature that can become an angel or a monster with just one amount.
